meta.name: HADS Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale
meta.description: The HADS is a 14-item self-report scale designed to detect anxiety and depression in medical outpatient settings. It avoids somatic symptoms that might be confused with physical illness, making it particularly suitable for hospital patients.
meta.author: Zigmond & Snaith (1983)
meta.reference: Zigmond AS, Snaith RP. The Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale. Acta Psychiatr Scand. 1983;67(6):361-370.
meta.instruction: Read each item and select the reply which comes closest to how you have been feeling in the past week. Your immediate reaction to each item will probably be more accurate than a long thought-out response.

questions.text: I feel tense or wound up
I still enjoy the things I used to enjoy
I get a sort of frightened feeling as if something awful is about to happen

choices.label: A little, but it does not worry me

questions.text: I can laugh and see the funny side of things

choices.label: As much as I always could

questions.text: Worrying thoughts go through my mind

choices.label: A great deal of the time

questions.text: I can sit at ease and feel relaxed
I feel as if I am slowed down
I get a sort of frightened feeling like butterflies in the stomach
I have lost interest in my appearance

choices.label: I do not take as much care as I should
I may not take quite as much care
I take just as much care as ever

questions.text: I feel restless as if I have to be on the move
I look forward with enjoyment to things

choices.label: As much as I ever did
Rather less than I used to
Definitely less than I used to

questions.text: I get sudden feelings of panic
I can enjoy a good book or radio or TV program

ranges.label: Normal
ranges.description: Your combined anxiety and depression scores are within the normal range. This indicates that your mood has been relatively stable recently, with no significant signs of persistent sadness or loss of interest.
suggestions: Continue maintaining good mental health
Keep up healthy habits and coping strategies
Maintain regular social activities and hobbies
Set aside time each day for activities you enjoy
Practice gratitude by noting positive moments in your life

ranges.label: Borderline
ranges.description: Your combined score suggests some level of emotional distress that may warrant monitoring. These mild symptoms may occasionally affect your energy and motivation, but they typically do not interfere significantly with daily functioning.
suggestions: Review the individual subscale scores for anxiety and depression
Consider consulting a healthcare provider if symptoms persist
Try 30 minutes of aerobic exercise daily, such as walking or cycling
Maintain a regular sleep schedule and avoid staying up late
Practice mindfulness meditation or deep breathing exercises

ranges.label: Abnormal
ranges.description: Your combined score indicates significant emotional distress. Professional evaluation is recommended. This indicates that your mood has been relatively stable recently, with no significant signs of persistent sadness or loss of interest.
suggestions: Seek professional help from a healthcare provider
Review subscale scores to identify whether anxiety, depression, or both are elevated
Maintain regular social activities and hobbies
Set aside time each day for activities you enjoy
Practice gratitude by noting positive moments in your life

anxiety.label: Normal
anxiety.description: Anxiety subscale score is within the normal range. This indicates that your anxiety levels are within the normal range, and your psychological state is relatively calm.
suggestions: Continue maintaining your healthy lifestyle and coping strategies
Practice daily relaxation activities such as deep breathing or stretching
Keep up a regular exercise routine
Cultivate mindful awareness habits
Maintain strong social connections

anxiety.label: Borderline Abnormal
anxiety.description: Borderline anxiety level. Monitoring is recommended. Mild tension or worry is common in everyday life. Simple self-regulation techniques can often help manage these feelings.
suggestions: Learn and practice progressive muscle relaxation
Try 5-10 minutes of mindfulness meditation daily
Reduce caffeine and stimulant intake
Record situations that trigger anxiety and try to analyze them rationally
Exercise regularly, at least three times a week

anxiety.label: Abnormal
anxiety.description: Clinically significant anxiety symptoms. Professional evaluation is recommended. This indicates that your anxiety levels are within the normal range, and your psychological state is relatively calm.
suggestions: Continue maintaining your healthy lifestyle and coping strategies
Practice daily relaxation activities such as deep breathing or stretching
Keep up a regular exercise routine
Cultivate mindful awareness habits
Maintain strong social connections

depression.label: Normal
depression.description: Depression subscale score is within the normal range. This indicates that your mood has been relatively stable recently, with no significant signs of persistent sadness or loss of interest.
suggestions: Maintain regular social activities and hobbies
Set aside time each day for activities you enjoy
Practice gratitude by noting positive moments in your life
Keep a consistent sleep schedule and exercise routine
Stay connected with friends and family

depression.label: Borderline Abnormal
depression.description: Borderline depression level. Monitoring is recommended. These mild symptoms may occasionally affect your energy and motivation, but they typically do not interfere significantly with daily functioning.
suggestions: Try 30 minutes of aerobic exercise daily, such as walking or cycling
Maintain a regular sleep schedule and avoid staying up late
Practice mindfulness meditation or deep breathing exercises
Stay in touch with trusted friends or family members
Reduce time spent alone and engage in social activities

depression.label: Abnormal
depression.description: Clinically significant depressive symptoms. Professional evaluation is recommended. This indicates that your mood has been relatively stable recently, with no significant signs of persistent sadness or loss of interest.
suggestions: Maintain regular social activities and hobbies
Set aside time each day for activities you enjoy
Practice gratitude by noting positive moments in your life
Keep a consistent sleep schedule and exercise routine
Stay connected with friends and family
